elements.sent-2411-001: We introduce and analyze a natural game formulated as follows.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this one-person game, the player is given a random permutation $A=(a_1,\dots, a_n)$ of a multiset $M$ of $n$ reals that sum up to $0$, where each of the $n!$ permutation sequences is equally likely.
elements.sent-2411-003: The player only knows the value of $n$ beforehand.
elements.sent-2411-004: The elements of the sequence are revealed one by one and the player can stop the game at any time.
elements.sent-2411-005: Once the process stops, say, after the $i$th element is revealed, the player collects the amount $\sum_{j=i+1 ^{n a_j$ as his/her payoff and the game is over (the payoff corresponds to the unrevealed part of the sequence).}}
elements.sent-2411-006: Three online algorithms are given for maximizing the expected payoff in the binary case when $M$ contains only $1$'s and $-1$'s.
elements.sent-2411-007: Moreover, it can also be used when $n$ is only known with some approximation.
elements.sent-2411-008: It turns out that the expected payoffs of the player are $\Theta(\sqrt{n )$ for all three algorithms.}
elements.sent-2411-009: In the end, we address the general problem and deal with an arbitrary zero-sum multiset, for which we show that our {\sc Algorithm 3 returns a payoff proportional to $\sqrt{n}$, which is worst case-optimal.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Stopping rules are of key importance in the study of random processes and/or online algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-011: Here is an illustrative example concerning the behavior of the largest payoff expectation in a game proposed by Leo Moser almost $70$ years ago~\cite{Mo56 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: A real number is drawn at random from $[0,1]$.
elements.sent-2411-013: We may either keep the number selected, or reject it and draw again.
elements.sent-2411-014: We can then either keep the second number chosen or reject it, and draw again, and so on.
elements.sent-2411-015: Suppose we have at most $n$ choices, and the payoff is the number we keep.
elements.sent-2411-016: What stopping rule gives the largest payoff expectation $E_n$ and how can we estimate $E_n$? Moser showed that, for any distribution, given at most $n$ choices, one should stop after the first choice if and only if the outcome is at least $E_{n-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-017: In particular, for the uniform distribution, $f_1(x)=1$ for $0 \leq x \leq 1$, $E_n$ satisfies the recurrence $E_{n+1 = \frac12 (1 + E_n^2)$, with $E_0=0$, $E_1=1/2$.}
elements.sent-2411-018: For illustration, we list some values in Table~\ref{tab:E_n .}
elements.sent-2411-020: The case of the normal distribution was studied by Guttman~\cite{Gu60 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Other examples of stopping rules, mostly from the area of selection, are mentioned in the ``Related work'' paragraph later in this section.. See also~\cite[Chap.~13]{MU17 .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \caption{$E_n$ in Moser's procedure for the uniform distribution (the first three digits).} \begin{center} \begin{tabular}{|c|c||c|c||c|c|} $n$ $E_n$ $n$ $E_n$ $n$ $E_n$ $1$ $.500$ $5$ $.775$ $100$ $.981$ $2$ $.625$ $10$ $.861$ $200$ $.990$ $3$ $.695$ $20$ $.919$ $300$ $.993$ \end{tabular} \end{center} \label{tab:E_n}
elements.sent-2411-022: Here we consider the following one-person game.
elements.sent-2411-023: Let $M$ be a (multi)set of $n$ reals that sum up to $0$.
elements.sent-2411-024: The value of $n$ is known to the player beforehand.
elements.sent-2411-025: The player is given a random permutation $A=(a_1,\dots,a_n)$ of $M$, where each of the $n!$ permutation sequences is equally likely.
elements.sent-2411-026: The elements of the sequence are revealed one by one and the player can stop the game at any step, \ie, after the $a_i$ is revealed, for any $i=1,2,\ldots,n$, or the stop is automatic at the end of the process.
elements.sent-2411-027: Once the process stops, say, after the $i$th element is revealed, the player collects the amount $\sum_{j=i+1 ^{n a_j$ as his/her payoff and the game is over.}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Note that the payoff corresponds to the unrevealed part of the sequence.
elements.sent-2411-029: Observe that any rational strategy for the game achieves a nonnegative outcome, since the player can always reduce its losses by continuing to play until the end, where he/she could force a zero payoff.
elements.sent-2411-030: In the binary case, i.e., if $n=2m$ and $M$ consists of $m$ copies of $-1$'s and $1$'s, respectively, we define the state of the process as the pair $(i,j)$, where $i$ and $j$ denote the numbers of $-1$ and $+1$, revealed so far, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-032: If the player stops the game before the first element (or after the last one) is revealed, his/her payoff is zero.
elements.sent-2411-033: If the player stops the game before the second element is revealed, his/her payoff is $1$ (the total amount of the unrevealed subsequence).
elements.sent-2411-034: Stopping the game at any other step would be irrational --- and is therefore omitted from discussion.
elements.sent-2411-035: The dual version of the game in which the payoff is the sum of the revealed values up to the stopping point admits a similar analysis.
elements.sent-2411-036: That is, if the player stops the game after the $i$th element of $A$ is revealed, his/her payoff is $\sum_{j=1 ^{i a_j$ (rather than $\sum_{j=i+1 ^{n a_j$).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: We present three online algorithms for maximizing the expected payoff in the binary case.
elements.sent-2411-038: Moreover, it can also be used when $n$ is only known with some approximation (in Section~\ref{sec:remarks ).}
elements.sent-2411-039: This algorithm requires quadratic preprocessing time but is otherwise very simple to use.
elements.sent-2411-040: Moreover, it is applicable to any zero-sum multiset.
elements.sent-2411-041: We show that the three algorithms share the same asymptotic performance, $\Theta(\sqrt{n )$, which is worst-case optimal.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Our results are summarized in the following.
elements.sent-2411-043: Let $M=\{a_1,a_2,\ldots,a_n\}$ be an arbitrary zero-sum multiset of reals.
elements.sent-2411-044: There is an online algorithm that reads a random permutation of $M$ and stops with an expected payoff of $\Omega(\mu \sqrt{n )$, where $\mu =\sum_{i=1 ^n |a_i|/n$ is the average absolute value for the input sequence.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: This bound is worst-case optimal, \ie, equality is attained (apart from the hidden constant factor) for a uniform sequence, with $n/2$ values equal to $\mu$ and $-\mu$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-046: The best choice problem for partially ordered sets considers a selection process in an online setting~\cite{GKMN08,Pr99 .}
elements.sent-2411-047: Let $X$ be a finite set equipped with a strict partial order $\prec$, \ie, an asymmetric and transitive relation.
elements.sent-2411-048: The objects (elements) of $X$ are shuffled --- each of the $|X|!$ permutations is equally likely --- and appear one by one before a selector $\S$, who must make a single online choice.
elements.sent-2411-050: The selector knows the cardinality $n=|X|$ but is ignorant of $\prec$.
elements.sent-2411-051: The selector's goal is to pick an element that is maximal with respect to $\prec$.
elements.sent-2411-052: That is, his/her goal is to stop the process at some time $\tau$ (that depends on the permutation) so that the currently examined element $x_t$ is maximal with respect to $\prec$.
elements.sent-2411-053: Such a random variable $\tau$ is called a stopping time.
elements.sent-2411-054: The question that arises is: (Q) Can the selector obtain a maximal element with probability at least $\delta$, for some constant $\delta$? --- irrespective of $(X,\prec)$.
elements.sent-2411-055: Preater~\cite{Pr99 was the first to obtain such an algorithm: the selector can pick a maximal element with probability at least $1/8$.}
elements.sent-2411-056: The authors of~\cite{GKMN08 improved the analysis of Preater's algorithm and showed that the success probability is at least $1/4$, which is tight for that algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-057: The case when $\prec$ is a total (\ie, linear) order, and thus known to $\S$, is the classic secretary problem~\cite{Fe89,LM85 .}
elements.sent-2411-058: In the language of online algorithms, one can refer to the problem as the online maximum problem: the elements in a random sequence $A$ of $n$ (distinct) numbers from an unknown distribution are revealed one by one, and the goal is to select the maximum by an irrevocable decision made immediately after examining the $i$th element for some $i=1,2,\ldots,n$.
elements.sent-2411-059: Each of the $n!$ permutations of the ranks is equally likely.
elements.sent-2411-060: Note that inaction leads to the automatic, \ie, forced, selection of the last (the $n$th) element in the sequence.
elements.sent-2411-061: The optimal algorithm achieves this task with a probability of success bounded from below by a constant, see~\cite[pp.~73--77]{Mo65 and~\cite[pp.~90--95]{LM85 .}}
elements.sent-2411-062: For large $n$, the optimal strategy passes approximately a fraction of $1/e$ of the sequence and chooses the first element that is larger every element of the initial prefix.
elements.sent-2411-063: Then the probability of success is approximately $1/e=0.367 \ldots$.
elements.sent-2411-064: Similar algorithms, whose probabilities of success are bounded from below by a constant, can be designed for finding the online minimum, online minimum and maximum, online smallest two and largest two, and for other variants.
elements.sent-2411-065: Under the additional assumption that the random numbers are uniformly distributed between $0$ and $1$, namely in Moser's problem described in the introduction, the probability of success of the optimal strategy is about $0.580$ (for large $n$); see~\cite[pp.~77--79]{Mo65 ,~\cite{Fe89 .}}
elements.sent-2411-066: The success of an algorithm for optimal selection can be also quantified by the rank of the selected element, where, say, the best rank is $1$ and the worst rank is $n$.
elements.sent-2411-067: Sixty years ago the authors of~\cite{CMRS64 obtained the elegant result that for the stopping rule which minimizes the expectation of the absolute rank of the selected element, as $n \to \infty$, the expected rank tends to the value of the infinite product $ \prod_{j=1 ^\infty \left( \frac{j+2 {j \right)^{\frac{1 {j+1 \approx 3.8695$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: A lattice path in $\ZZ^2$ is a path that moves right or up by one unit at each step.
elements.sent-2411-069: Consider the integer lattice points in the square $[0,m] \times [0,m]$ and the set $L_m$ of lattice paths connecting the lower left corner of the square $(0,0)$ with the upper right corner of the square $(m,m)$.
elements.sent-2411-070: In the binary case, the evolution of the game corresponds to a prefix of a path in $L_m$.
elements.sent-2411-071: The path moves a step to the right if a revealed element is $-1$, and moves a step up if it is $+1$.
elements.sent-2411-072: If the player stops the game after the $i$th element is revealed ($i \geq 1$), the path length is $i$.. See Fig.~\ref{fig:paths .}

Here $n=6$. The line $y=x$ is in blue.} \label{fig:paths}
elements.sent-2411-073: Recall the following result regarding lattice paths --- obtained by the so-called reflection principle:.
elements.sent-2411-074: The number of lattice paths from $(0,0)$ to $(m,m)$ that reach line $\ell: y=x-t$ is ${2m \choose m-t}$.
elements.sent-2411-075: Let $E_t$ be the event that a random lattice path in $L_m$ reaches line $\ell: y=x-t$.
elements.sent-2411-076: Recall that the total number of paths in $L_m$ is $|L_m|={2m \choose m}$.
elements.sent-2411-077: Then by Lemma~\ref{lem:reach we obtain: \begin{equation}\label{eq:reach} \Prob\left(E_t\right) = \frac{{2m \choose m-t}}{{2m \choose m}}.\end{equation}.}
elements.section-2411-003: A simple threshold algorithm for the binary case
elements.sent-2411-078: An integer threshold value $t =\Theta(\sqrt{n )$ is set; where $n=2m$.}
elements.sent-2411-079: The algorithm waits until the lattice path reaches the line $\ell: y=x-t$, if at all, when it stops the game.
elements.sent-2411-080: If the lattice path describing the evolution of the game doesn't reach the line $\ell$, the algorithm continues until the end (\ie, the entire input sequence is revealed).
elements.sent-2411-081: Next we proceed with the details.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: [H] \DontPrintSemicolon \TitleOfAlgo{Algorithm 1} \KwIn{the elements of a zero-sum sequence $A$ of length $2m$ over $\Sigma=\{-1,+1\}$ are revealed one by one} Set $t = \left \lfloor \sqrt{\frac{m+1}{2}} \right \rfloor$\; Follow the lattice path from $(0,0)$ to $(m,m)$ ($\rightarrow$ or $\uparrow$) as dictated by the entries of $A$ that have been revealed (step by step) by repeating Step 3\; If the path has reached the line $\ell: y=x-t$ stop; else continue\;
elements.sent-2411-082: We first deduce a lower bound on the expected payoff.
elements.sent-2411-084: From~\eqref{eq:reach we further obtain by applying the standard inequality $1-x \geq \e^{-2x}$ for $0 \leq x \leq 1/2$ in the last step: \begin{equation}\Prob\left(E_t\right) = \frac{{2m \choose m-t}}{{2m \choose m}} = \frac{m(m-1) \cdots (m-t+1)}{(m+t)(m+t-1) \cdots (m+1)} \nonumber = \prod_{i=1}^{t} \left( 1 - \frac{t}{m+i} \right) \label{eq:prod1} \geq \left(1 - \frac{t}{m+1} \right)^t \geq \exp \left(\frac{-2t^2}{m+1} \right) \label{eq:prod2} \geq \exp(-1). \nonumber\end{equation} Let $W_1$ denote the expected payoff taken by the player.}
elements.sent-2411-085: For the above value of $t$ we have: \begin{equation}\label{eq:prod3} W_1 = t \cdot \Prob\left(E_t\right) \geq \frac{1}{\e} \left \lfloor \sqrt{\frac{m+1}{2}} \right \rfloor = \Omega(\sqrt{m}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-086: As for the upper bound, observe that the payoff is at least $t$ with probability only at most $\Prob\left(E_t\right)$ even if the player has complete information about a given random sequence, and chooses the best moment to stop the game.
elements.sent-2411-087: Hence, the expected payoff of any algorithm is at most the sum of these probabilities (see~\cite[Lemma~2.9]{MU17 ): \begin{equation}\sum_{t=1}^{m}\Prob\left(E_t\right) = \sum_{t=1}^{m}\frac{{2m \choose m-t}}{{2m \choose m}} = \frac{2^{2m-1}}{{2m \choose m}}-\frac{1}{2} = \frac{\sqrt{\pi m }}{2}-\frac{1}{2}+O(m^{-1/2}),\end{equation} where the last equality is a routine series expansion.}
elements.sent-2411-089: Executing one step of Algorithm 1 takes constant time.
elements.sent-2411-090: Our findings apply with some adjustments to the following variants:.
elements.sent-2411-091: The player is required to stop the game before the last element in the sequence is revealed.
elements.sent-2411-092: Since the state of the process is known at any step, the player can always reduce its losses, if any are perceived at the current step, by continuing to play until exactly one element, the last one, remains, where he/she can stop the game and so the loss can be limited to $-1$.
elements.sent-2411-093: The analysis of this variant is similar.
elements.sent-2411-094: The length $n$ of the input sequence is only known approximately, say, $N \leq n \leq 2N$, for a given $N$.
elements.sent-2411-095: For this variant, the player can use the {\sc Algorithm 1 with a similar expected performance, where $n$ is replaced by $N$.}
elements.section-2411-004: An optimal algorithm for the binary case
elements.sent-2411-096: The algorithm constructs two square matrices, whose content is described below.
elements.sent-2411-097: Let $\left[ T[i,j] \right]_{0 \leq i,j \leq m}$ be a $(m+1)\times (m+1)$ matrix with nonnegative entries, where $T[i,j]$ is the expected value of the player's payoff if he/she starts playing optimally in state $(i,j)$, \ie, after $i$ elements of $-1$ and $j$ elements of $+1$ have been revealed.
elements.sent-2411-098: As such, $T[0,0]$ is the expected value of the game in an optimal play.
elements.sent-2411-099: The optimal strategy is encoded in the stopping square matrix $S$ as described below.
elements.sent-2411-100: Let $\left[ S[i,j] \right]_{0 \leq i,j \leq m}$ be a $(m+1)\times (m+1)$ matrix with 0-1 entries, where \begin{equation}S[i,j] =  1 if stop at this step --- in state  (i,j), 0 if continue at this step --- in state  (i,j). \end{equation} The matrix $T$ is constructed in a backwards manner by using dynamic programming.
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}S[i,j] =  1 if stop at this step --- in state  (i,j), 0 if continue at this step --- in state  (i,j). \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-101: Indeed, the last row and column of $T$ are known beforehand: $T[i,m]=0$, for $i=0,1,\ldots,m$, and $T[m,j]=m-j$ for $j=0,1,\ldots,m$.
elements.sent-2411-102: Assuming that the entries $T[i+1,j]$ and $T[i,j+1]$ are known, the entry $T[i,j]$ can be computed as follows: First, the transition probabilities $p_0$ and $p_1$ to reach states $(i+1,j)$ and $(i,j+1)$, respectively, by revealing a new element in state $(i,j)$ are computed: \begin{equation}\label{eq:p0p1} p_0 = \frac{m-i}{2m-i-j}, ~~~ p_1 = \frac{m-j}{2m-i-j}.\end{equation}. Then \begin{equation}\label{eq:max} T[i,j] = \max \left( i-j, p_0 \cdot T[i+1,j] + p_1 \cdot T[i,j+1] \right).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}\label{eq:max} T[i,j] = \max \left( i-j, p_0 \cdot T[i+1,j] + p_1 \cdot T[i,j+1] \right).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-103: As such, the entries of $T$ can be computed row by row, from top to bottom, and each row from right to left.
elements.sent-2411-104: Consider an entry $T[i,j]$; If the maximum in~\eqref{eq:max is given by the first term, $i-j$, the corresponding entry in $S$ is set to $1$: $S[i,j]=1$.}
elements.sent-2411-105: Indeed, this means that the expected payoff if the algorithm continues from state $(i,j)$ does not exceed the payoff available in state $(i,j)$ if the algorithm stops.
elements.sent-2411-106: Otherwise, if the maximum in~\eqref{eq:max is given by the second term, set $S[i,j]=0$.}
elements.sent-2411-107: Some of the stopping entries are unreachable (for example, in the last column), but this is not an issue.
elements.sent-2411-108: In summary, the algorithm works as follows:.
elements.algorithm-2411-002: [H] \DontPrintSemicolon \TitleOfAlgo{Algorithm 2} \KwIn{the elements of a zero-sum sequence $A$ of length $2m$ over $\Sigma=\{-1,+1\}$ are revealed one by one} Compute the matrices $T$ and $S$ as specified above\; Follow the path in $S$ starting from the entry $(0,0)$ by repeating Step 3\; If $S[i,j]=1$ (\ie, if $(i,j)$ is a stopping state), stop; else continue to the next entry in $S$ ($S[i+1,j]$ or $S[i,j+1]$) as dictated by the most recently revealed element of~$A$\;
elements.sent-2411-109: Two examples with the optimal strategy as computed by Algorithm 2 appear in Fig.~\ref{fig:optimal .}

Right: an optimal strategy for $n=20$; to avoid the clutter, only the reachable stopping states are shown. Note that all stopping states are below the main diagonal (with one exception, the zero entry for the automatic stop in the end).} \label{fig:optimal}

elements.sent-2411-121: Let $W_2$ be the expected payoffs of the player under {\sc Algorithm 2 .}
elements.sent-2411-122: Since {\sc Algorithm 2 is optimal, it is stronger than {\sc Algorithm 1 from Section~\ref{sec:simple , and thus $W_2 \geq W_1 =\Omega(\sqrt{n )$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-123: In addition, the general upper bound analysis in Section~\ref{sec:simple yields that every algorithm, including {\sc Algorithm 2 , has a payoff at most $O(\sqrt{n )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-125: With a shuffled standard deck of $52$ cards ($n=52$), the player using Algorithm~2 is guaranteed \$2.62 in expectation (which is optimal), if the payoff is, say, the number of black cards minus the number of red cards in what is left unturned from the deck, with \$1 per card.
elements.sent-2411-126: For comparison, the player using Algorithm~1 is guaranteed \$1.54 in expectation, by~\eqref{eq:prod3 (however, no optimization was attempted in its analysis).}
elements.section-2411-005: A `Stop in the Middle' algorithm for the binary case
elements.sent-2411-127: In this section we offer yet another algorithm for the binary case, Algorithm 3.
elements.sent-2411-128: Its expected payoff is $\Omega(\sqrt{n )$, asymptotically the same as for the previous two algorithms.}
elements.sent-2411-129: This algorithm is extremely simple as it does not perform any bookkeeping.
elements.sent-2411-130: However, the main reason for presenting it is its applicability to arbitrary zero-sum sequences as shown in Section~\ref{sec:middle-g .}
elements.sent-2411-131: For convenience, here we formulate the algorithm for the dual version of the game, in which, if the player stops the game after the $i$th element of $A$ is revealed, his/her payoff is $\sum_{j=1 ^{i a_j$.}}
elements.sent-2411-132: Consider the function $g \colon \RR \to \RR$ defined as \begin{equation}g(x) = \begin{cases} x \text{ if } x>0 0 \text{otherwise.} \end{cases}\end{equation} The algorithm's payoff is $g(\sum_{j=1 ^{n/2 a_j)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-133: Note that this only requires computing a running sum up to index $n/2$ and making a single sign determination.
elements.algorithm-2411-003: [H] \DontPrintSemicolon \TitleOfAlgo{Algorithm 3} \KwIn{the elements of a zero-sum sequence $A$ of length $n$ are revealed one by one} Compute the running sum $S=\sum_{j=1}^{n/2} a_j$ step by step\; If $S>0$ stop; else continue to the end \;
elements.sent-2411-134: Assume for convenience in the analysis that $n=4m$.
elements.sent-2411-135: Then it is easy to see that in the binary case ($\Sigma=\{-1,+1\}$), the total number of zero-sum sequences is ${4m \choose 2m}$, whereas there are exactly ${2m \choose m-k {2m \choose m+k}$ of them for which the payoff is $2k$, $0 \le k \le m$.}
elements.sent-2411-136: Hence, the expected payoff $W_3(n)$ is \begin{equation}\label{eq:alg3} W_3(n) = {4m \choose 2m}^{-1} \sum_{k=1}^m 2k {2m \choose m-k}{2m \choose m+k}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-137: A few values of $W_3(n)$ for some small even $n$ are listed in Table 2.
elements.sent-2411-138: The following standard identity, which we prove in Appendix~\ref{sec:app , leads to a closed form expression of the expected payoff.}
elements.sent-2411-139: Now a routine series expansion yields \begin{equation}W_3(n) = 2m{2m-1 \choose m-1}^2 {4m \choose 2m}^{-1} = \sqrt{\frac{m}{2\pi}} + O(m^{-1/2}) = \sqrt{\frac{n}{8\pi}} + O(n^{-1/2}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-140: Therefore, $W_3(n) =\Theta(\sqrt{n )$, asymptotically the same as for the previous two algorithms.}
elements.section-2411-006: The `Stop in the Middle' algorithm for the general case
elements.sent-2411-141: In this section we generalize our results to an arbitrary zero-sum multiset $M=\{a_1,\ldots,a_n\}$ and thereby prove Theorem~\ref{thm:main .}
elements.sent-2411-142: Assume for convenience that the input size $n$ is even (the treatment for the case of odd $n$ is similar, but somewhat more involved, and is therefore omitted).
elements.sent-2411-143: As in Section~\ref{sec:middle-b , here we consider the dual version of the game, in which the payoff is the sum of the revealed elements.}
elements.sent-2411-144: We show that the expected payoff achieved by Algorithm 3 on a random permutation of $M$ is $f(M) =\Omega(\mu \sqrt{n )$, where $\mu =\sum_{i=1 ^n |a_i|/n$ is the average absolute value for the input multiset.}}
elements.sent-2411-145: Let $n_+$ and $n_-$ denote the number of positive and respectively negative entries in $M$; where $n=n_+ + n_-$.. Let $m=n/2$.
elements.sent-2411-146: Note that $f(\cdot)$ is a linear function, \ie, $f(\lambda M) = \lambda f(M)$, where $\lambda M = \lambda \{a_1,\ldots,a_n\} = \{\lambda a_1, \ldots, \lambda a_n\}$.
elements.sent-2411-147: The key lemma below shows that the expected payoff can only decrease when replacing two distinct elements by two copies of their average.
elements.sent-2411-148: If the two elements are both positive or both negative, this type of replacement maintains $n_+$ and $n_-$.
elements.sent-2411-149: If the two elements have different signs, the replacement can be used to adjust the counts $n_+$ and $n_-$, as illustrated after the proof of the lemma.
elements.sent-2411-150: Let $a,b \in M$, $a<b$, be two distinct elements of $M$ and $M'= M \setminus \{a,b\}$.. Then \begin{equation}f\left(M' \cup \{a,b\}\right) \geq f\left(M' \cup \left\{\frac{a+b}{2}, \frac{a+b}{2}\right\}\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-151: Let $F \subset M$ denote the first half of the random input permutation of $M$.
elements.sent-2411-152: If both $a,b \in F$, or both $a,b \notin F$, then the corresponding payoffs satisfy the required inequality (as an equality, since $a+b = \frac{a+b {2 + \frac{a+b {2}$).}}}
elements.sent-2411-153: Suppose now that exactly one element of $\{a,b\}$ appears in $F$, and let $x$ denote the sum of the other $m-1$ elements in $F$.
elements.sent-2411-154: Note that $a$ precedes $b$ with probability $1/2$ and vice-versa, so it remains to check that \begin{equation}\label{eq:inequality} \frac12 g(x+a) + \frac12g(x+b) \geq g\left(x + \frac{a+b}{2}\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-156: Consider the four possible intervals where $x$ can lie in: $x \leq -b$, $-b \leq x \leq -\frac{a+b {2}$, $-\frac{a+b {2 \leq x \leq -a$, $-a \leq x$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-157: In each of these cases, the verification of~\eqref{eq:inequality is straightforward:.}
elements.sent-2411-158: If $M$ satisfies $n_+=n_-=n/2$, we invoke Lemma~\ref{lem:replace with a pair of distinct elements of $M$ that are either both positive or both negative, while maintaining the zero-sum property and the average absolute value $\mu$.}
elements.sent-2411-159: It is not hard to see that $f(\cdot)$ is continuous as a function of a given $n$-element multiset.
elements.sent-2411-160: Therefore, repeated invocation of Lemma~\ref{lem:replace allows us to average all the elements of $M$ of same sign by compactness.}
elements.sent-2411-161: In other words, we have $f(M) \geq f(B)$, where $B$ is a `binary' multiset consisting of two elements $\mu$ and $-\mu$, each with multiplicity $n/2$.
elements.sent-2411-163: If $M$ fails to satisfy $n_+=n_-=n/2$, we assume without loss of generality that $n_-<n_+$ and proceed as follows.
elements.sent-2411-164: On each step, we pick $a$ and $b$ as the most negative and the least positive elements of $M$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-165: We have that $|a|>b$ by the zero-sum property.
elements.sent-2411-166: One application of Lemma~\ref{lem:replace increases $n_-$ by one, and reduces $n_+$ by one while maintaining the zero-sum property.}
elements.sent-2411-167: Note, however, that this operation decreases the average absolute value $\mu$.
elements.sent-2411-168: After $(n_+-n_-)/2$ such steps, we get a multiset $M'$ that satisfies $n_+=n_-=n/2$.
elements.sent-2411-169: Since \begin{equation}n_+-\frac{n_+-n_-}{2} > \frac{n_+}{2}\end{equation} of the largest positive elements of the initial multiset $M$ remain in $M'$, its average absolute value $\mu'$ satisfies $\mu'>\mu/2$ by the zero-sum property.
elements.sent-2411-170: Moreover, the argument from the previous paragraph yields that $f(M') = \Omega(\mu'\sqrt{n )$.}
elements.sent-2411-171: Therefore, we conclude that $f(M) \ge f(M') = \Omega(\mu\sqrt{n )$, as desired.}
elements.sent-2411-173: In the first step, we average $-5$ with $+1$ to balance the number of positive and negative elements.
elements.sent-2411-174: Then we `average' the elements of the same sign in arbitrary order.
elements.sent-2411-176: Zero-sum sequences are relevant in the study of dynamic or random processes and/or optimization under uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-177: We formulate two questions for further investigation.
elements.sent-2411-178: The first one is more technical but its solution may offer additional insight on the problem.
elements.sent-2411-179: The second one appears to be interesting and motivating for real applications, such as simulation or trading.
elements.sent-2411-180: Can the entries of $T$ describing the payoffs of an optimal strategy for zero-sum sequences over $\Sigma=\{-1,+1\}$ be derived in closed form?.
elements.sent-2411-181: Can a better expected payoff be guaranteed in the general case, by taking advantage of the specificity of the input?.
elements.sent-2411-182: Our argument relies on a standard Vandermonde identity, see~\cite[Section~5.2]{GKP90 , \begin{equation}\sum_{i=0}^{r}\binom{s}{i}\binom{t}{r-i}=\binom{s+t}{r},\end{equation} and a handful of simple algebraic manipulations: \begin{equation}\sum_{k=1}^m 2k {2m \choose m-k}{2m \choose m+k} = m\sum_{k=0}^m 2{2m \choose m-k}{2m \choose m+k} -2\sum_{k=0}^{m-1} (m-k) {2m \choose m-k}{2m \choose m+k} = m{2m \choose m}^2+m\sum_{k=-m}^m {2m \choose m-k}{2m \choose m+k}-2m\sum_{k=0}^{m-1} 2{2m-1 \choose m-k-1}{2m \choose m+k} = m{2m \choose m}^2+m\sum_{i=0}^{2m} {2m \choose i}{2m \choose 2m-i}-2m\sum_{k=0}^{m-1} 2{2m-1 \choose m-k-1}{2m-1 \choose m+k-1} -2m\sum_{k=0}^{m-1} 2{2m-1 \choose m-k-1}{2m-1 \choose m+k} =m{2m \choose m}^2+m{4m \choose 2m}-2m\sum_{k=-m}^{m-1} {2m-1 \choose m-k-1}{2m-1 \choose m+k-1} -2m{2m-1 \choose m-1}^2-2m\sum_{k=-m+1}^{m-1}{2m-1 \choose m-k-1}{2m-1 \choose m+k} =m{2m \choose m}^2+m{4m \choose 2m}-2m\sum_{i=0}^{2m-1} {2m-1 \choose i}{2m-1 \choose 2m-1-i} -2m{2m-1 \choose m-1}^2-2m\sum_{i=0}^{2m-2}{2m-1 \choose i}{2m-1 \choose 2m-2-i} =m{2m \choose m}^2-2m{2m-1 \choose m-1}^2 +m{4m \choose 2m} -2m{4m-2 \choose 2m-1} -2m{4m-2 \choose 2m-2} =m{2m \choose m}^2-2m{2m-1 \choose m-1}^2 = 2m{2m-1 \choose m-1}^2.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-016: \begin{equation}\sum_{k=1}^m 2k {2m \choose m-k}{2m \choose m+k} = m\sum_{k=0}^m 2{2m \choose m-k}{2m \choose m+k} -2\sum_{k=0}^{m-1} (m-k) {2m \choose m-k}{2m \choose m+k} = m{2m \choose m}^2+m\sum_{k=-m}^m {2m \choose m-k}{2m \choose m+k}-2m\sum_{k=0}^{m-1} 2{2m-1 \choose m-k-1}{2m \choose m+k} = m{2m \choose m}^2+m\sum_{i=0}^{2m} {2m \choose i}{2m \choose 2m-i}-2m\sum_{k=0}^{m-1} 2{2m-1 \choose m-k-1}{2m-1 \choose m+k-1} -2m\sum_{k=0}^{m-1} 2{2m-1 \choose m-k-1}{2m-1 \choose m+k} =m{2m \choose m}^2+m{4m \choose 2m}-2m\sum_{k=-m}^{m-1} {2m-1 \choose m-k-1}{2m-1 \choose m+k-1} -2m{2m-1 \choose m-1}^2-2m\sum_{k=-m+1}^{m-1}{2m-1 \choose m-k-1}{2m-1 \choose m+k} =m{2m \choose m}^2+m{4m \choose 2m}-2m\sum_{i=0}^{2m-1} {2m-1 \choose i}{2m-1 \choose 2m-1-i} -2m{2m-1 \choose m-1}^2-2m\sum_{i=0}^{2m-2}{2m-1 \choose i}{2m-1 \choose 2m-2-i} =m{2m \choose m}^2-2m{2m-1 \choose m-1}^2 +m{4m \choose 2m} -2m{4m-2 \choose 2m-1} -2m{4m-2 \choose 2m-2} =m{2m \choose m}^2-2m{2m-1 \choose m-1}^2 = 2m{2m-1 \choose m-1}^2.\end{equation}
